key_insight: The paper proposes a structured distributionally robust optimization framework that makes learned inverse-problem reconstructions robust to realistic distribution shifts by perturbing only the relevant parts of the data-generation process.
engineering_relevance: This enables more stable and interpretable reconstruction models for imaging systems such as deblurring and CT, especially when test-time noise or forward physics differ from training assumptions.
method: Restrict the ambiguity set to structured perturbations aligned with the forward model, including joint, marginal, or conditional distributions.
Derive strong duality and finite-dimensional dual forms for the robust objective.
Show that the worst-case risk yields a Tikhonov-style penalty on the Lipschitz constant of the reconstructor.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical experiments on deblurring and sinogram-to-CT reconstruction, where the proposed method outperforms standard DRO and MSE baselines in robustness, stability, and interpretability.
watch_item: The framework’s benefits depend on how well the chosen structured ambiguity set matches the true acquisition process; an overly restrictive or misspecified set may limit robustness or complicate implementation.
paper_title: A Distributionally Robust Framework for Learned Reconstructions in Inverse Problems
